narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the unique challenges and strengths you bring from your background. The admissions model evaluated your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 25.50, Year 1 school decile of 5, Year 3 decile of 3, upper-middle family income, and plan to attend full-time, which is the typical path for most students. It predicted a 54% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try—meaning it's slightly more likely than not that you might struggle to pass, tipping the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which is well below the average of 36.70 seen in the dataset, signaling to the model a higher risk compared to typical admitted students. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since your 2.80 is reasonably close to the average of 3.21, showing solid academic potential. Your Year 3 decile of 3, however, hurt your case more than your Year 1 decile of 5 helped, as the drop suggested inconsistent performance relative to peers, with averages around 5.5 for both. Factors like your upper-middle family income and full-time status provided smaller boosts toward success, aligning with the majority of students who succeed. Overall, your profile stands out as lower than typical in key academic predictors like LSAT and later-year rankings, which outweighed the positives for this model. While this is disappointing, it doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your perspective as a Black woman in law. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
